figures.caption: Figure 9: Kodak dataset for reference. Each image has a resolution of either 512× 768 or 768× 512. In this work, kodim18 and kodim21 are employed as target images for high-resolution GSM.

Figure 2: Overview of reconstruction fidelity attacks. Rec and Adv are abbreviations for reconstruction and adversarial, respectively. (a) Untargeted distortion induced by PGD. (b) LSM from [1], with the manipulated region highlighted by a white box. (c) Low-resolution GSM from [1]. (d) Our high-resolution GSM. The proposed minimal yet effective PGD2-GSM enables stable ℓ∞-bounded high-resolution GSM in LIC systems via our Periodic Geometric Decay step-size schedule.
Figure 5: Visual comparison between PGD2-GSM and PGD with different step sizes.

Table 2: Performance of PGD2-GSM for high-resolution GSM under standard and defense settings. In the Attack column, w/o and with denote whether the differentiable JPEG approximation (Q = 90) is used for generating adversarial examples. In the Inference column, they denote whether JPEG (Q = 90) is applied as a transformation defense during reconstruction.
Figure 6: Quantitative comparison between PGD2-GSM and PGD with different step sizes.

Figure 12: Reconstructions of high-resolution GSM examples. In the parentheses, the first w/o denotes that high-resolution GSM examples are generated directly on the LIC model, while with indicates generation on the cascaded differentiable JPEG and LIC model. The second w/o denotes reconstruction without JPEG, whereas with applies a JPEG transformation prior to reconstruction. The source image is indicated on the left of each image group. The target image is either kodim18 or kodim21, depending on the resolution.
Figure 11: Visualizations of adversarial examples generated by PGD2-GSM under different perturbation budgets and iteration numbers. The source and target images are Kodim16 and Kodim21, respectively.
Figure 7: Quantitative results of PGD2-GSM under different T and ϵ.
Table 3: Ablation study of PGD2-GSM on the decay factor k.
Figure 10: Visualizations of high-resolution GSM examples generated by PGD and PGD2-GSM.
